medications.indication_rationale: The offending agent. It must be CEASED immediately, not continued — ongoing statin exposure (recently doubled to a high dose and amplified by the interacting antibiotic) is driving the muscle toxicity. The AI Doctor should advise stopping it now and arrange prompt medical review; it does not itself prescribe.
contraindications_in_this_case: active severe myopathy with high rhabdomyolysis risk
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: clarithromycin (macrolide) — inhibits CYP3A4, markedly increasing atorvastatin exposure and the risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis
medications.deprescribing_note: Currently atorvastatin 80 mg daily (recently doubled). Cease immediately and do not restart until reviewed; the reviewing clinician will decide on future lipid management once recovered.

medications.indication_rationale: The interacting drug. It should be stopped because, taken with the statin, it inhibits statin metabolism and drives toxicity. If ongoing antibiotic treatment for the respiratory infection is still required, a prescriber should substitute a non-interacting agent.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: atorvastatin — CYP3A4 inhibition raises statin levels and myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise stopping the macrolide; substitution with a non-interacting antibiotic (if still indicated) is a prescriber decision made at the in-person review.

medications.drug_name: non-macrolide antibiotic (e.g. a penicillin such as amoxicillin) if ongoing treatment of the respiratory infection is still required
medications.indication_rationale: If the chest infection still needs treatment, replacing the macrolide with a non-interacting antibiotic avoids the statin interaction. The AI Doctor does NOT prescribe or set a dose — this is flagged for the prescriber at the same-day review. No dose is provided here.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the cholesterol tablet now and hold the current antibiotic until reviewed today; bring all medication boxes to the in-person review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Immediate cessation of both interacting drugs halts ongoing muscle toxicity and lets the clinician confirm exactly what has been taken.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch urine colour and how much urine is passed; report immediately any darkening (brown/tea/cola) or a marked drop in urine output.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dark urine or reduced output is an early warning of rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury and mandates emergency care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain good oral fluid intake as advised by the reviewing clinician (the source clinician advised around 2.5 litres of water daily short-term); confirm the target with the clinician, as fluid needs vary in older adults.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Adequate hydration supports renal protection during muscle breakdown, but the volume should be individualised by a clinician in a frail older adult.

patient_education_points.point: Your severe muscle aching and weakness is very likely a reaction to your cholesterol medicine, made worse because it is being taken together with your current antibiotic.
Stop the cholesterol tablet now, hold the antibiotic, and get seen in person today so you can have blood tests to check a muscle enzyme (CK) and your kidney function.
Go straight to an emergency department if your urine turns dark brown or tea-coloured, you pass much less urine, your weakness gets much worse, or you start vomiting.
Use a firm, high-backed chair with armrests and have someone help you move, to avoid a fall while your leg strength is poor.
Muscle pain and weakness usually settle over a few weeks once the offending medicine is stopped, but you need monitoring to be sure your kidneys and muscles recover.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person medical review; repeat CK and renal function within 24-72 hours as advised by the reviewing clinician
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: CK trend, renal function and electrolytes, symptom improvement after stopping the statin, the full medication list and antibiotic substitution, and falls/mobility safety at home
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: dark or tea-coloured urine, reduced urine output, worsening or spreading weakness, vomiting, confusion, or any collapse — go to emergency care immediately

must_include_items: Recognise that severe symmetrical proximal muscle pain and weakness is likely a statin-related muscle toxicity, not benign strain
Elicit and act on BOTH the recent statin dose increase and the concurrent antibiotic, identifying the drug-drug interaction
Advise immediate cessation of the statin and stopping/holding the interacting antibiotic (with prescriber substitution if still needed)
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment with CK and renal-function bloods
Give clear rhabdomyolysis/AKI safety-netting (dark urine, reduced output, worsening weakness, vomiting -> emergency department)
Give falls-prevention advice given the loss of independent mobility
acceptable_alternatives: Escalating directly to urgent care or an emergency department for the CK/renal bloods if a same-day GP appointment and pathology are not available (especially after hours).
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise stopping the statin
Failing to recognise or act on the interacting macrolide antibiotic
Failing to arrange urgent CK and renal-function testing
Failing to safety-net for rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury
Under-triaging to routine review or reassurance
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient that this is benign muscle strain and can wait
Advising the patient to continue the statin
Inventing a specific drug dose (including for any replacement antibiotic)
Recommending simple analgesia and rest without escalation or bloods
Recommending unsupervised high-volume fluids in a frail older adult without clinician oversight
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) advising cessation of the statin, (b) recognising the statin-antibiotic interaction and advising the antibiotic be stopped/replaced by a prescriber, and (c) arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment with CK/renal bloods plus rhabdomyolysis/AKI safety-netting. Missing (a), (b) or the urgent escalation is a critical failure regardless of other content.
